userMessage: check scope and tell me waht can u infer

response.text:
Good, I have a fresh live screenshot. Here's everything I can infer from the full scope display:

---

## Scope Setup
- **Channel:** Ch 1
- **Scale:** 500 mV/div
- **Input impedance:** 1 MΩ
- **Bandwidth:** 1 THz (scope bandwidth limit is off / max)
- **Time base:** 100 ns/div
- **Acquisition mode:** Single — this is a **one-shot capture**, not a continuous rolling average
- **Date/Time:** 26 Mar 2026, 3:47 PM

---

## Measurement Table — What the Numbers Say

| Measurement | Mean | Min | Max | Std Dev | Population |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency** | 7.855 MHz | 2.352 MHz | 13.358 MHz | 7.783 MHz | 2 |
| **Eye High** | 3.158 V | 3.067 V | 3.226 V | 68.5 mV | 7 |
| **Eye Low** | 388 mV | 46.9 mV | 1.686 V | 608 mV | 17 |
| **Eye Width** | 24.835 ns | 24.835 ns | 24.835 ns | 0 s | 1 |
| **Rise Time** | 8.146 ns | 7.893 ns | 8.473 ns | 297 ps | 3 |

### What those numbers tell us:
1. **Frequency varies wildly** — min 2.35 MHz, max 13.36 MHz, std dev 7.78 MHz on only 2 samples. This is **not a fixed-rate clock**. It is a data stream with irregular pulse widths.
2. **Eye High is tight and consistent** — only 68 mV std dev. The logic-high level is stable and well-driven.
3. **Eye Low is a disaster** — 608 mV std dev, ranging from 47 mV to 1.686 V across 17 samples. The low level is extremely inconsistent. Some "lows" are barely below the midpoint. This is the biggest problem on this signal.
4. **Eye Width has zero variance** — only 1 sample. Not statistically meaningful yet.
5. **Rise time is consistent** — 297 ps std dev across 3 measurements. The rising edge is repeatable, just slow at ~8 ns.

---

## Waveform View — What the Trace Tells Us

- **Acquisition markers A1–A4** are visible (teal boxes). These are search/measurement anchor regions the scope placed automatically.
- **A1 and A4** (far left and far right) are **failed markers (red X)** — the scope could not make a valid measurement there.
- **A2 and A3** (middle) are **passed markers (green check)** — valid measurements were taken.
- The **trigger point (T)** is at ~0 ns, right at the first rising edge.
- **90%/10% markers** are shown on a rising edge — that's the rise time measurement window.
- The waveform shows **pulses of varying width** — some wide (~200 ns), some narrow (~50 ns), confirming it is data, not a clock.
- The **long high-time pulse** from ~500–700 ns shows the signal fully settling to ~3.2 V when given enough time.
- Short pulses never fully reach 3.2 V — they cap out lower, confirming **RC/bandwidth-limited settling**.

---

## Eye Diagram — What It Shows

- **Mid-level: 1.658 V** — the scope's auto-detected crossing threshold
- **UIs: 24/24, Total: 24/24** — 24 unit intervals captured, all used
- The eye is **wide open** but the traces are **thick and curved**, not tight and sharp
- The **crossing region is spread** — horizontal jitter is visible
- The top of the eye is relatively tight; the **bottom half is messy and spread** — consistent with the Eye Low std dev of 608 mV
- Two distinct "levels" are visible in the lower half of the eye, suggesting the low level depends heavily on the preceding bit pattern

---

## Overall Inference — Plain English

| Aspect | Verdict |
|---|---|
| Signal type | Irregular digital data stream, NOT a clock |
| Logic high | Clean and stable ✅ |
| Logic low | Highly variable, problematic ⚠️ |
| Transitions | Slow but consistent rise time |
| Signal integrity | Degraded — bandwidth/RC limited |
| Eye open? | Yes, but with reduced margin |
| Acquisition | Single-shot, small population — not statistically rich yet |

**Most important finding:** The low level is the weak point. With a std dev of 608 mV and a max Eye Low of 1.686 V, some logic-zero symbols are sitting at nearly **half the supply voltage**. Any receiver with a threshold above ~800 mV could misread those as a logic-one. That is a real risk for reliable decoding.
